EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.

A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.2 inches 5 miles north northwest of Holstein and 1.8 inches 3 miles northeast of Battle Creek.  Winds at Sioux City frequently gusted from 25 to 35 mph with a peak of 38 mph, resulting in visibility of less than a mile, and briefly one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from southwest Iowa to north central Iowa Saturday afternoon. Storms developed, moved across a warm front, and four tornadoes touched down over eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  A few other storms brought small hail and damaging wind to portions of the region Saturday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Jesup, Iowa about 615 PM CDT, March 28th. The tornado moved northeast and caused damage to outbuildings and a barn, and uprooted trees at a farmstead. The tornado was on the ground for about 10 minutes, and traveled a little more than 7 miles. The tornado was rated EF-1, with maximum winds around 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet end to the 3rd week of June and continued moderate to heavy rainfalls into the middle of the 4th week of June caused rivers across the eastern portions of the Des Moines CWA to swell and reach flood stage from as little as a few hours near Tama to a few days around Cedar Falls, Waterloo, and Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Waterloo crested at 15.54 feet at 0415 UTC on June 25th. Flood stage is 13.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal across northwest Iowa, varying from 10 to 25 percent of normal in the far northwest corner, to around 50 percent of normal toward Ida Grove and Storm Lake. Temperatures were mainly near to slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions early in the month. Rainfall of less than 50 percent of normal along with near normal temperatures contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late on the evening of October 11th a line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front that passed across Iowa. This was a high shear, low CAPE environment with MLCAPE of 500 to 1200 J/kg and deep layer shear of 40 to 50 knots. This environment with steep low level lapse rates and DCAPE of 1000+ J/kg yielded a damaging wind threat. While no damage reports were received, many observation stations reported gusts over 60 mph. Storms exited the area to the east in the early morning hours of October 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS recorded a 63 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher flows from rainfall and snow melt were sustained by heavy rainfall again at the end of March and beginning of April. A drier period heading into mid-month allowed the river to fall below flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of minor flooding from March, the Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) began the month 0.83 feet above flood stage, with a slow and steady fall to below flood stage on April 3. Main impacts occurred with flooding of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave came off of the Rocky Mountains out over the central Plains. This led to a surface low developing over Kansas. In front of that surface low, warm moist air was advected north. Isentropic upglide and led to an overrunning precipitation event that led to some high snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. It was the #5 (5.4 inches) and #4 (4.5 inches) highest April snowfall events for Omaha and Lincoln respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public sent in report of 6 inches of snow in Council Bluffs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS developed in western NE and western KS and came through southwest Iowa in the early morning hours producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several tree limps snapped off and damage to the roof of a 40 foot silo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initially unimpressive thunderstorm complex over northeast Nebraska rapidly intensified during the morning of August 10, 2020. After a few sporadic early morning damage reports, the intensifying storms began to more readily produce damaging winds in excess of 50 kts around 1340Z. Those damaging winds increased in coverage and intensity as the storms moved east into Iowa. The portion of the thunderstorm complex that moved through the NWS Omaha forecast area marked the approximate beginning of an intense derecho that tracked across portions of Iowa, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Michigan, and Ohio. Within the NWS Omaha forecast area, there were more than 20 local storm reports of wind damage and measured severe wind gusts, including more than 50,000 power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop Observer estimated winds of 70 mph. Noted that several trees were down in his area.

The portion of the thunderstorm complex that moved through the NWS Omaha forecast area marked the approximate beginning of an intense derecho that tracked across portions of Iowa, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Michigan, and Ohio. Within the NWS Omaha forecast area, there were more than 20 local storm reports of wind damage and measured severe wind gusts, including more than 50,000 power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public personal weather station measured a wind gust of 50 kts.

Heavy rain from thunderstorms the evening of March 27th resulted in flash flooding in portions of far eastern Iowa. This preceded the severe weather event which happened Saturday, March 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a warm front across portions of northeast Iowa during the late afternoon of March 28th. These storms spawned two tornadoes that touched down in Fayette County. The first tornado moved through the western sections of Oelwein, damaging apartment buildings and numerous trees in Red Gate Park and a nearby cemetery. One of the damaged apartment buildings was condemned. The second tornado damaged several farm buildings near Maynard. The storms also produced some large hail with quarter sized hail reported in Elkader (Clayton County) and Monona (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Fayette. This was likely the remnants of the Maynard tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed and another injured when two semi's collided southeast of Monona (Clayton County) in dense fog. The driver of one semi failed to stop at an intersection of U.S. Highway 52 and State Highway 13 and collided with the second semi. One of the trucks was transporting lead-acid batteries that were strewn across the roads. A hazard materials team had to be brought in for clean up, requiring the roads to be closed for much of the day. The visibility at the time of the accident was likely reduced to less than a half mile due to dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed and another injured when two semi's collided at the intersection of U.S. Highway 52 and State Highway 13 southeast of Monona. The driver of one semi failed to stop at the intersection and collided with the second semi. Lead-acid batteries were strewn across the roads as a result of the accident. A hazard materials team had to be called in for clean up, resulting in the roads being closed for several hours. The visibility was likely reduced to less than a half mile in dense fog at the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary lifting north through Iowa was the focus for convection and tornado development.  Sufficient instability and enhanced shear near the boundary helped produce seven tornadoes on this day.  One tornado occurred in Waukee, Iowa but produced only EF0 damage.  The strongest tornado was an EF1 located south of Eagle Grove reaching a width of almost 250 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed on the north edge of downtown Waukee and moved northwest, causing minor damage at a church and car dealership.  The tornado also removed some shingles and roofing to a new house that was still under construction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning convection across southern Iowa developed on the nose of the low-level jet in conjunction with a stationary front, remaining sub-severe. This boundary lifted north into central Iowa and by afternoon CAPE exceeded 2000 J/kg with 30+ knots of shear. Storms were able to restrengthen along this boundary in the afternoon, producing up to baseball-sized hail and gusty winds resulting in tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter report of golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave came off of the Rocky Mountains out over the central Plains. This led to a surface low developing over Kansas. In front of that surface low, warm moist air was advected north. Isentropic upglide and led to an overrunning precipitation event that led to some high snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. It was the #5 (5.4 inches) and #4 (4.5 inches) highest April snowfall events for Omaha and Lincoln respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS report in Council Bluffs for 6.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast Iowa started April with some river flooding along portions of the Mississippi River. The flooding was the result of runoff from snow that melted in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melted snow pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in McGregor. The river crested over two feet above the flood stage at 18.38 feet on April 7th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melted snow pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Guttenberg. The river crested almost two feet above the flood stage at 16.76 feet on April 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of June 26th. These storms dropped some hail near Lime Springs (Howard County) that ranged in size from a nickel up to a quarter. These storms also produced wind damage just north of Lime Springs where trees and power lines were blown down and the doors to a metal machine shed were blown onto the roof of the shed. A private weather station measured a 67 mph wind gust in Waucoma (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 65 mph wind gust occurred in Lime Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen west of Saratoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and storms developed over portions of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of June 23rd. A funnel cloud formed with the activity near Cresco (Howard County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Cresco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air advection lifting across the Missouri River Valley and into Iowa was enough to induce scattered thunderstorms across southern Iowa in the afternoon. While instability was rather weak, stronger shear values helped to organize convection. Most storms remained sub-severe however a few were strong enough to produce severe hail in southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of pea to nickle sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system that produced severe storms the previous day across southern Iowa was pushed from the area as strong kinematic forcing associated with a cold front pushed across northwest Iowa. This caused temperatures to drop rather quickly  with robust snow totals across parts of northwest Iowa. A few locations measured nearly a foot of snow.

A shortwave moving across the Rockies experienced lee-cyclogenesis into the central plains. As the low tracked south of the state isentropic lift across Missouri and into southern Iowa. Frontogenetical forcing allowed for a band to set up across southern Iowa where several location reported ten to twelve inches of snow. Further north towards the I-80 corridor lesser amounts were recorded with some changeover to rain.

This system resulted in a prolonged winter event with snow beginning across southern Iowa before expanding across central and northern Iowa, where the bulk of the snow fell. Initial dry air reduced ice introduction and portions of southern Iowa saw minor ice accumulations before transitioning over to snow. As the system lifted across central and northern Iowa snow was the predominant precipitation type. A strong cold front on the back side of the system as precipitation was coming to an end brought strong winds across much of northwest Iowa, resulting in blizzard conditions as freshly fallen snow was easily lofted, significantly reducing visibility. Widespread snow totals of 5 to 8 inches were seen across much of Iowa.||While many areas did not meet the traditional winter storm warning criteria strictly using snowfall, strong winds after snowfall ended combined with the fresh snow to provide near blizzard conditions at times during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th in areas mainly south and east of the upgraded Blizzard Warning area.

A winter storm spread light to moderate snow across across northeast Missouri, eastern Iowa, and far northwest Illinois late Friday morning into the afternoon.  Snow changed over to sleet and freezing rain during the evening hours across most of the area.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 0.5 inches across west central Illinois to 5.5 inches in far northwest Illinois and area of Iowa north of Interstate 80.  Freezing rain reports ranged from a tenth to a quarter of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official NWS observation at the Davenport Municipal Airport was 4.5 inches of snow and 2 tenths of an inch of freezing rain. An off duty NWS employee reported 5.3 inches of snow in Bettendorf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stubborn cold air remained near the surface while strong warm advection ahead of a mid-level trough resulted in a distinct above-freezing layer.  The result was a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, which produced a glaze of ice on objects and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain resulted in ice accumulation to around a tenth of an inch, with roads becoming extremely slick across southern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stubborn cold air remained near the surface while strong warm advection ahead of a mid-level trough resulted in a distinct above-freezing layer.  The result was a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, which produced a glaze of ice on objects and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain resulted in a glaze of ice accumulation, before light snowfall  brought up to 1.6 inches of snowfall to 3 miles northeast of Battle Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brisk winds in advance of the center of strong arctic high pressure brought wind chill readings to -25 to -35 during the overnight and morning of January 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coldest wind chill readings reached -32F around 0730CST January 19 near Storm Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system traversed the area in the early morning hours of January 15. Lacking deep saturation, most precipitation fell as light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, intermixed with a variety of solid types at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation fell prior to and during the morning commute time. Roads quickly became ice-covered.  Shortly after precipitation ended, around 1040CST, a northbound semi-truck lost control and crossed the center line on U.S. Highway 59 about a mile south of Primghar.  A southbound vehicle attempted to avoid a collision, but was hit by the semi, which spun and rolled the vehicle in the west ditch, fortunately without injury to the driver. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This system resulted in a prolonged winter event with snow beginning across southern Iowa before expanding across central and northern Iowa, where the bulk of the snow fell. Initial dry air reduced ice introduction and portions of southern Iowa saw minor ice accumulations before transitioning over to snow. As the system lifted across central and northern Iowa snow was the predominant precipitation type. A strong cold front on the back side of the system as precipitation was coming to an end brought strong winds across much of northwest Iowa, resulting in blizzard conditions as freshly fallen snow was easily lofted, significantly reducing visibility. Widespread snow totals of 5 to 8 inches were seen across much of Iowa.||While many areas did not meet the traditional winter storm warning criteria strictly using snowfall, strong winds after snowfall ended combined with the fresh snow to provide near blizzard conditions at times during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th in areas mainly south and east of the upgraded Blizzard Warning area.

Stubborn cold air remained near the surface while strong warm advection ahead of a mid-level trough resulted in a distinct above-freezing layer.  The result was a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, which produced a glaze of ice on objects and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain resulted in ice accumulation to nearly two tenths of an inch. County officials recommended no travel for several hours during the morning.  Light snowfall followed with 0.5 inches at Spencer and 3 miles west northwest of Everly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system traversed the area in the early morning hours of January 15. Lacking deep saturation, most precipitation fell as light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, intermixed with a variety of solid types at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix fell prior to and during the morning commute. Roads quickly became ice-covered and dangerous with several accidents reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stubborn cold air remained near the surface while strong warm advection ahead of a mid-level trough resulted in a distinct above-freezing layer.  The result was a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, which produced a glaze of ice on objects and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain resulted in ice accumulation to around a tenth of an inch, with a change to light snowfall which brought a dusting to 0.2 inches at Cherokee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stubborn cold air remained near the surface while strong warm advection ahead of a mid-level trough resulted in a distinct above-freezing layer.  The result was a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, which produced a glaze of ice on objects and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of precipitation brought ice accumulation to around two tenths of an inch along with a dusting of light snowfall, with one half inch accumulation at Sioux City and 1.0 inch at Holly Springs.  Ten accidents were reported around the Sioux City metro area, along with numerous vehicles unable to climb untreated hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.11 inches at Lake Park, 1.06 inches 3 miles south-southeast of Lake Park and 0.90 inches at Spirit Lake, the Little Sioux River above Spencer Highway 18 (gage site SPII4) returned to flood at the end of the month. The river crested at 0.41 feet above flood stage on March 30, resulting in minor flooding of agricultural land north of Spencer.  The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

A strong cold front on the back side of the system as precipitation was coming to an end brought strong winds across much of northwest Iowa, resulting in blizzard conditions as freshly fallen snow was easily lofted, significantly reducing visibility. Widespread snow totals of 5 to 8 inches were seen across much of Iowa.||While many areas did not meet the traditional winter storm warning criteria strictly using snowfall, strong winds after snowfall ended combined with the fresh snow to provide near blizzard conditions at times during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th in areas mainly south and east of the upgraded Blizzard Warning area.

A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day.  Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa.  Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initially light snowfall became heavy at times during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Intense snowfall rates resulted in a rapid worsening of road conditions. Accumulation reached 7.0 inches at Orange City, 6.5 inches at Alton and 4.0 inches at Sioux Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system brought heavy precipitation of 1 to 2 inches to areas along and east of the Mississippi River January 10 into January 11.  Much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois saw a wintry mix of sleet, snow, freezing rain and freezing drizzle.  Slick roads were common, with several accidents reported throughout the region.  There were numerous reports of ice accumulations between a tenth and a quarter of an inch.  Some trees were downed from the weight of the ice and numerous power outages were reported.  Snowfall amounts were generally between 1 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Lowden reported a quarter of an inch of ice and 1.5 inches of snow. Shortly after midnight on January 11, 2020 a tractor trailer slid off of Interstate 80 and fell over on its side near West Branch, Iowa or near mile marker 255. A passenger in the tractor trailer was killed. The crash was caused by icy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest on January 17th and 18th. The storm generally dropped 3 to 5 inches of snow across the area followed by a light coating of ice from freezing drizzle. Despite the snow becoming ice crusted, wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph broke up the ice and snow, producing blowing snow and blizzard conditions from the morning of the 18th through much of the afternoon. The visibility was reduced to around a quarter of a mile during the blizzard conditions with drifting snow impacting most roads. The highest recorded wind gust was 45 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 4 inches of snow fell across Fayette County. A period of freezing drizzle created a coating of ice on top of the snow. Some blowing and drifting snow occurred during the morning and afternoon of the 18th with wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph. The highest recorded wind gust was 45 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.

A weak system traversed the area in the early morning hours of January 15. Lacking deep saturation, most precipitation fell as light freezing rain or freezing drizzle, intermixed with a variety of solid types at times.

The visibility was reduced to around a quarter of a mile during the blizzard conditions with drifting snow impacting most roads. The highest recorded wind gust was 45 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions occurred across Mitchell County during the late morning and afternoon of January 18th. The visibility was reduced to around a quarter mile in blowing snow as wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph occurred. Prior to the blizzard conditions, a period of winter storm conditions occurred from the afternoon of the 17th into the morning of the 18th. Around 4 inches of snow fell along with a coating of ice from freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This system resulted in a prolonged winter event with snow beginning across southern Iowa before expanding across central and northern Iowa, where the bulk of the snow fell. Initial dry air reduced ice introduction and portions of southern Iowa saw minor ice accumulations before transitioning over to snow. As the system lifted across central and northern Iowa snow was the predominant precipitation type. A strong cold front on the back side of the system as precipitation was coming to an end brought strong winds across much of northwest Iowa, resulting in blizzard conditions as freshly fallen snow was easily lofted, significantly reducing visibility. Widespread snow totals of 5 to 8 inches were seen across much of Iowa.||While many areas did not meet the traditional winter storm warning criteria strictly using snowfall, strong winds after snowfall ended combined with the fresh snow to provide near blizzard conditions at times during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th in areas mainly south and east of the upgraded Blizzard Warning area.

A sharp upper-level trough amplified as it dug through the Northern Plains on January 3. Intensifying low-level wind gradients resulted in a burst of stronger winds for a couple hours, which accompanied snowfall to produce a brief period of dangerous visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.6 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford and 1.2 inches near Lake Park. Strong northerly winds gusted as high as 38 mph near Estherville and 44 mph at Spencer, and resulted in a brief period of visibility as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp upper-level trough amplified as it dug through the Northern Plains on January 3. Intensifying low-level wind gradients resulted in a burst of stronger winds for a couple hours, which accompanied snowfall to produce a brief period of dangerous visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 1.1 inches at Spencer.  Despite the lesser accumulation of snow, strong northerly winds gusted as high as 44 mph at Spencer and resulted in a brief period of visibility as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day.  Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa.  Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was briefly moderate to heavy during the afternoon and early evening, accumulating to 5.3 inches at Hartley, 5.0 inches at Sanborn, 4.7 inches at Primghar and 3.6 inches at Sheldon, resulting in dangerous travel conditions.  Poor road conditions lingered after the snowfall due to continued drifting snow with increased southerly winds. On January 13 around 1620CST, a vehicle lost control attempting to pass another on U.S. Highway 18 around 2 miles east of Sanborn, entered the ditch and rolled, resulting in an (indirect) injury to the driver.  A second accident around 0900CST the following day and in approximately the same location resulted in a rollover in the ditch and another (indirect) injury. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17. A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow. By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa. Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes. Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle. Ice accumulations were generally a glaze to a tenth of an inch, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening. Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts during the morning reaching 45 mph at Luverne and 35 mph at Doon.  Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less, with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 6.1 inches at Rock Rapids.

Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less, with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 7.0 at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17. A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow. By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa. Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes. Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle. Ice accumulations were generally a glaze to a tenth of an inch, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening. Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts during the morning reaching 53 mph at Spencer. Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less, with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 7.6 inches at Spencer and 7.0 inches 3 miles west northwest of Everly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This system resulted in a prolonged winter event with snow beginning across southern Iowa before expanding across central and northern Iowa, where the bulk of the snow fell. Initial dry air reduced ice introduction and portions of southern Iowa saw minor ice accumulations before transitioning over to snow. As the system lifted across central and northern Iowa snow was the predominant precipitation type. A strong cold front on the back side of the system as precipitation was coming to an end brought strong winds across much of northwest Iowa, resulting in blizzard conditions as freshly fallen snow was easily lofted, significantly reducing visibility. Widespread snow totals of 5 to 8 inches were seen across much of Iowa.||While many areas did not meet the traditional winter storm warning criteria strictly using snowfall, strong winds after snowfall ended combined with the fresh snow to provide near blizzard conditions at times during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th in areas mainly south and east of the upgraded Blizzard Warning area.

Severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front that dropped south across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Tuesday evening.  Heavy downpours and very large hail were reported. Widespread quarter sized hail was also reported during the evening in several other counties. ||This was the 1st of back to back severe hail events, as another significant event occurred April 8th, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season snow storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of southeast Iowa, extreme northeast Missouri and northwest Illinois April 16 and 17. With warm ground temperatures, there were not many impacts to travel other than reduced visibility at times, and minor slushy accumulations on roads. The higher amounts of 5 to 8 inches fell across the Fairfield and Keosauqua areas eastward into Des Moines County in southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 4.5 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer 4 miles north of Marengo to 5.0 inches from a COOP Observer 1 mile east of Williamsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across Iowa through the afternoon serving as a focus for convection. MUCAPE of 1000-1500 J/kg and strong deep-layer shear were sufficient for storm development in the warm sector. Storms were fast-moving with storm motions of 45 mph, but fortunately, most storms remained below severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported nickel sized hail with 40-50 mph winds resulting in small tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level system over the Ozarks lifted north and interacted with a northern stream shortwave moving across the area, amplifying forcing.  High precipitable water values of nearly two inches were in place. While the system was somewhat progressive in nature, an area of enhanced reflectivity set up over east-central to northeast Iowa, resulting in prolonged heavy rain in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP report of 3 inches of rain in 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid to late May rainfall across portions of northeast Iowa kept soil moisture high and river levels generally elevated. Heavier rainfalls around the Waterloo area and to the north, including portions of the Cedar River basin, overnight on the 27th into the morning of the 28th allowed the Cedar River to reach flood stage for just over 24 hours from 0045 UTC on the 30th to 0315 UTC  on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Cedar Falls crested at 89.44 feet on May 30th at 1215 UTC or 615am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning convection across southern Iowa developed on the nose of the low-level jet in conjunction with a stationary front, remaining sub-severe. This boundary lifted north into central Iowa and by afternoon CAPE exceeded 2000 J/kg with 30+ knots of shear. Storms were able to restrengthen along this boundary in the afternoon, producing up to baseball-sized hail and gusty winds resulting in tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS report of 2.56 inches of rain. Beginning and end time roughly estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.11 inches at Lake Park and 0.90 inches at Spirit Lake, the Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) returned to flood at the end of the month. The river crested at 0.89 feet above flood stage on March 31, resulting in flooding of crop and pasture land. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified moving from southeast Colorado on the morning of March 19, into the Great Lakes early on March 20.  A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.  Sioux City reached record precipitation for the date with 1.58 inches on March 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall by late afternoon and early evening on March 19, with visibility briefly one-quarter to one-half mile at times between 2000-2300CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the evening as winds increased to a peak of 43 mph near Maurice and 41 mph at Alton. Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Hull and 2.0 inches at Rock Valley and Orange City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough across the central U.S. shifted east, lifting a surface low and associated cool front across the area. With CAPE under 1000 J/kg storms remained below severe criteria, however the slow progression of the system resulted in heavy rain, mainly across northern Iowa where wrap around rain on the back of the system resulted in two to three inches of rain by the next morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP report of 3.34 inches of rain. Beginning and end time roughly estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Six tornadoes occurred across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The tornadoes remained in rural areas with some damage to farm buildings and trees.  No injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed just south of the Adams/Adair county line moving northeast.  The tornado encountered a farmstead producing EF0 damage to an outbuilding.  The northeast trajectory took the tornado into Adair county shortly after impacting the farmstead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado moved into Adair county from Adams county.  The tornado remained in rural southern Adair county as it moved to the northeast.  It did encounter one farmstead and produced EF1 damage to an outbuilding along with additional tree damage.  The rest of the track was in rural cropland before dissipating several miles south of Fontanelle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCV developed overnight across central Kansas and lifted into Iowa later in the day where it encountered instability of 3000+ J/kg. Low-level CAPE and helicity values, in addition to low LCLs, resulted in an increased tornado threat. While storms remained below severe limits, funnel clouds were reported in far southern and northern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple public reports of a funnel cloud near Clear Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Six tornadoes occurred across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The tornadoes remained in rural areas with some damage to farm buildings and trees.  No injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture of tornado near Voorhies was received from trained spotter.   The path of the tornado was later discovered in high resolution satellite imagery and adjusted accordingly.  The tornado remained in rural farm fields although passed very close to a house near the intersection of State Highway 175 and Highway 63, although no damage was reported.  With no damage occurring to any damage indicators, the tornado was rated an EFU.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Six tornadoes occurred across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The tornadoes remained in rural areas with some damage to farm buildings and trees.  No injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado developed in rural farmland between Hudson and Waterloo moving quickly northeast.  The tornado encountered an building site destroying a hay shelter building that was open on both sides.  Additional damage occurred to a small storage building and trees on the site.  All damage was rated EF0.  The tornado continued northeast and dissipated before reaching Highway 20 on the south side of Waterloo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary remained fairly stationary across the state with environmental parameters continuing to support storm development. Storms across central Iowa produced some wind damage, downing trees and snapping a power pole. A tornado also occurred, cutting through Johnston, a northern Des Moines suburb. Tornado damage was largely confined to trees and powerlines with no structural damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began near Terra Park and ended near Dewey Park in Johnston.  Estimated peak wind speed of 95 mph was determined with damage to multiple large trees and power lines.  No damage to buildings was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary lifting north through Iowa was the focus for convection and tornado development.  Sufficient instability and enhanced shear near the boundary helped produce seven tornadoes on this day.  One tornado occurred in Waukee, Iowa but produced only EF0 damage.  The strongest tornado was an EF1 located south of Eagle Grove reaching a width of almost 250 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser caught a brief tornado in rural Boone county.  The tornado did not impact any damage indicators, hence the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level system over the Ozarks lifted north and interacted with a northern stream shortwave moving across the area, amplifying forcing.  High precipitable water values of nearly two inches were in place. While the system was somewhat progressive in nature, an area of enhanced reflectivity set up over east-central to northeast Iowa, resulting in prolonged heavy rain in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WOI replayed a public report of 2.25 inches of rain. Beginning and end time roughly estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall during the week of May 13-20 totaled 3.40 inches at Lake Park, 2.97 inches at Spirit Lake and 2.77 inches 3 miles south southeast of Lake Park.  Upper portions of the Little Sioux River returned briefly into minor flood, with some moderate flooding near Milford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River near Milford (gage site MIFI4) spent much of the last half of the month in minor flood, but briefly climbed into moderate flooding with a crest of 2.25 feet above flood stage on May 21.  Significant amounts of agricultural and pasture land near the river was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a frontal zone pushing eastward across South Dakota and Nebraska. With sufficient elevated instability and deep-layer shear, a local enhancement in the larger-scale bow echo developed and produced a small area of damaging winds in extreme southeast South Dakota and adjacent northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six-inch diameter limb was broken off a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall between May 23 and May 27 totaled 2.22 inches 3 miles west northwest of Everly and 1.97 inches near Spencer.  Runoff resulted in minor flooding along the Ocheyedan River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ocheyedan River near Spencer (gage site SPOI4) crested 0.55 feet above flood stage on May 27. Agricultural land west of Spencer was flooded.

Six tornadoes occurred across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The tornadoes remained in rural areas with some damage to farm buildings and trees.  No injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado was caught on video by a storm chaser.  The chaser was located just south of the Highway 20 and Highway 218 intersection looking northward toward downtown Waterloo.  The tornado occurred in an unpopulated area southeast of downtown near the Waterloo Waste Water Treatment Plant along the Cedar River.  It is likely some tree damage occurred, however no reports were received, therefore the tornado was rated EFU.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Estherville was in flood stage from 8am 1400 UTC to 1930 UTC including a crest of 10.69 at 1715 UTC.

The winter snow pack across Minnesota and Wisconsin was 125 to 200 percent of normal. This slowly melted during the spring and caused the Mississippi River in Burlington, Iowa to rise above its major flood stage during the second week of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Burlington (BRLI4) rose above its major flood stage of 18 feet at 9 PM CST on April 1 and fell below major flood stage at 1 AM on April 16th. The river crested at 18.21 feet at 6 AM on April 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.11 inches at Lake Park and 0.88 inches at Spencer, the Little Sioux River at Spencer (gage site SPWI4) returned above flood stage at the end of the month. The river crested 1.04 feet above flood stage on March 30, and remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

A late season snow storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of southeast Iowa, extreme northeast Missouri and northwest Illinois April 16 and 17. With warm ground temperatures, there were not many impacts to travel other than reduced visibility at times, and minor slushy accumulations on roads. The higher amounts of 5 to 8 inches fell across the Fairfield and Keosauqua areas eastward into Des Moines County in southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 4.0 inches from a COOP Observer 1 mile east northeast of Fairfield to 8.5 inches from a trained spotter 1 mile west of Perlee. Most of the accumulation was on grassy surfaces with less accumulation on sidewalks and roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a strong trough digging into the Northern Plains brought a period of very strong winds, gusting as high as 40 to 50 mph, with a period of light snowfall. While accumulations were generally less than an inch, the areas with heavier snow from February 8-9 experienced an extended period of ground blizzard conditions from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph at Sheldon, when combined with new snowfall of one-half inch or less, produced visibility of less than one mile for several hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph at Sioux City, when combined with new snowfall of less than one-half inch, produced visibility briefly less than one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front out of the northern plains pushed south across the midwest resulting in rapidly dropping temperatures. In some cases, sites saw temperature drops of 15 to 20 degrees over the course of an hour or so and very strong northwesterly winds ushered in arctic air. The rapid drop in temperatures also induced a flash freeze scenario for much of the state where liquid on area roadways from light rain and melting earlier in the day quickly froze and the front swept through.

The rapid drop in temperatures also induced a flash freeze scenario for much of the state where liquid on area roadways from light rain and melting earlier in the day quickly froze and the front swept through.

An arctic front associated with a strong trough digging into the Northern Plains brought a period of very strong winds, gusting as high as 40 to 50 mph, with a period of light snowfall. While accumulations were generally less than an inch, the areas with heavier snow from February 8-9 experienced an extended period of ground blizzard conditions from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph 1 mile north northeast of Sibley, when combined with new snowfall of an inch or less, produced visibility of less than one mile for several hours.

A winter storm moved rather quickly across the region on February 9th. This storm produced a period of heavy snow with rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour at times. Accumulations across portions of northeast Iowa were generally in the 5 to 8 inch range. The highest reported amounts were 8.5 inches near Bluffton (Winneshiek County) and 7.8 inches west of Dorchester (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across Mitchell County. The highest reported total was 6 inches in Carpenter.

A wet end to the 3rd week of June and continued moderate to heavy rainfalls into the middle of the 4th week of June caused rivers across the eastern portions of the Des Moines CWA to swell and reach flood stage from as little as a few hours near Tama to a few days around Cedar Falls, Waterloo, and Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Hawk Creek at Hudson crested at 1530 UTC on June 23rd at 18.12 feet. Flood stage is 15.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet end to the 3rd week of June and continued moderate to heavy rainfalls into the middle of the 4th week of June caused rivers across the eastern portions of the Des Moines CWA to swell and reach flood stage from as little as a few hours near Tama to a few days around Cedar Falls, Waterloo, and Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa River near Tama created at 1445 UTC on June 25th at 12.53 feet. Flood stage is 12.50 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough digging into the Northern Plains established a tight mid-level frontal band which focused snowfall as it traversed the region. The band remained nearly stationary for 6 to 9 hours from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Instability led to further contraction of the frontal band with a very intense gradient from little snow to a half-foot or more. Isolated thundersnow was reported across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Briefly moderate to heavy snowfall accumulated to 3.1 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford and 2.3 inches at Spirit Lake.

A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.

With one to two inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near the Iowa Great Lakes, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper portion of the Little Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River at Spencer (gage site SPWI4) rose above flood stage on March 6, and crested 1.70 feet above flood stage on March 10. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to two inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near the Iowa Great Lakes, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper portion of the Little Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) rose briefly above flood stage and crested 0.43 feet above flood stage on March 7. Impacts were primarily inundation of lower-lying agricultural lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm temperatures and well below normal rainfall led to the development of drought conditions in portions of west central and central Iowa in July.  These conditions slowly expanded in area through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Acute short term dryness led to the development of severe drought conditions in much of Adair county during the later half of the month of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of July 11th. These storms dropped quarter sized hail across portions of Chickasaw and Fayette Counties. The hail damaged some crops east of Lawler (Chickasaw County). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.

With one to two inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near the Iowa Great Lakes, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper portion of the Little Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River above Spencer Highway 18 (gage site SPII4) rose above flood stage on March 7, and crested 1.44 feet above flood stage on March 10. Significant amounts of agricultural land were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and remnants of a nocturnal mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) slowly moved across Iowa in the late morning to early afternoon hours. With moderate CAPE in the 1500-3000 J/kg range and shear of 20-30 kts, conditions were sufficient to support severe storm development. Storms produced winds gusting up to 70 mph at times, leaving behind significant tree damage in a few locations across north central and east central Iowa, along with periods of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS measured a 53 knot wind gust at the Mason City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large longwave trough was in place across much of the western and central US with an embedded shortwave. This provided a focus for storm development as it moved into a humid and unstable environment. Scattered convection eventually increased in coverage through the evening, producing quarter sized hail and damaging wind gusts. As convection grew upscale and moved into southern Iowa the severe threat diminished, though heavy rain continued to fall through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report via social media of 2 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the  middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Brandon (gage site BBSS2) crested 1.23 feet above flood stage on March 15. Significant flooding of agricultural lands occurred upstream and downstream of Brandon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave embedded in northwest flow moved across Iowa, providing a focus from storm development in an environment that already had MLCAPE values of 2500-3000 J/kg and effective bulk shear of around 40 knots. Convection that occurred earlier in the morning of July 11th also provided a focus for storm development on lingering outflow boundaries. This allowed for swaths of large hail across the state, with reports of up to tennis ball sized hail in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports via social media of a funnel cloud southeast of Pocahontas and southwest of Gilmore City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the  middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.

A line of thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of July 11th. These storms dropped quarter sized hail across portions of Chickasaw and Fayette Counties. The hail damaged some crops east of Lawler (Chickasaw County). The storms also produced some damaging winds that blew down trees west of Randalia (Fayette County). Some farm out buildings were blown down and corn flattened northeast of Westgate (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several farm out buildings were damaged or blown down and some corn was flattened northeast of Westgate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across much of northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of July 9th. Damaging winds from these storms blew down trees in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), Oelwein (Fayette County) and blew a roof of a building in West Union (Fayette County). Trees and a power lines were blown down in Postville  and Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large longwave trough was in place across much of the western and central US with an embedded shortwave. This provided a focus for storm development as it moved into a humid and unstable environment. Scattered convection eventually increased in coverage through the evening, producing quarter sized hail and damaging wind gusts. As convection grew upscale and moved into southern Iowa the severe threat diminished, though heavy rain continued to fall through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported a 24 hour rain total of 2.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across much of northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of July 9th. Damaging winds from these storms blew down trees in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), Oelwein (Fayette County) and blew a roof of a building in West Union (Fayette County). Trees and a power lines were blown down in Postville  and Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were blown down in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Non-severe morning convection was ongoing on the morning of July first. Though this diminished through the day as the associated surface front weakened, some redevelopment did occur during the afternoon. As the upper level low moved across eastern Iowa, enough surface vorticity existed to produce a brief funnel cloud with a shower in east central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report and picture of funnel cloud with an approximate location near Malcom south of interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level ridge resulted in warm and humid conditions across the area with modest uncapped instability. While there was little in the way of forcing mechanism to develop storms, high frequency gravity wave could be seen in satellite imagery moving through the flow in the cumulus field. A handful of short-lived showers developed from this, and with sufficient low level instability in place, a few funnel clouds developed with these showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement relayed multiple reports of funnel clouds occurring with showers in the area.

As convection grew upscale and moved into southern Iowa the severe threat diminished, though heavy rain continued to fall through the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and remnants of a nocturnal mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) slowly moved across Iowa in the late morning to early afternoon hours. With moderate CAPE in the 1500-3000 J/kg range and shear of 20-30 kts, conditions were sufficient to support severe storm development. Storms produced winds gusting up to 70 mph at times, leaving behind significant tree damage in a few locations across north central and east central Iowa, along with periods of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported trees down across Algona.

A mid-level short wave came off of the Rocky Mountains out over the central Plains. This led to a surface low developing over Kansas. In front of that surface low, warm moist air was advected north. Isentropic upglide and led to an overrunning precipitation event that led to some high snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. It was the #5 (5.4 inches) and #4 (4.5 inches) highest April snowfall events for Omaha and Lincoln respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer in Clarinda, IA reported 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across central Iowa into eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and northwest Illinois late Friday afternoon and into the overnight hours. These storms brought heavy rain, frequent lightning, damaging winds, and large hail to the region.  The hardest hit areas were Keokuk county in Iowa, towards Henry county in Iowa, where 50-70 mph winds occurred.  A weak EF-1 tornado occurred near Atkinson, IL in Henry county in Illinois after 1015 pm and caused damage to trees and outbuildings at two farmsteads. ||Widespread rainfall amounts over 1 inch generally fell south of a line from Sigourney and Maquoketa, IA to Freeport, IL.  This rainfall combined with the previous days rain brought rain totals of 2-4 inches to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported very strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains.  An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation.  More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon.  Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle overnight and much of the morning accumulated to one to two tenths of an inch, especially on elevated surfaces, including powerlines which resulted in brief power outages.  Precipitation changed to snow during the midday and afternoon, and totaled 3.7 inches in Lake Park and 3.1 inches 4 miles northwest of Milford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, briefly mixed with sleet or freezing drizzle during the morning, became heavy at times between 1300-1600CST. Slick roads caused a school bus carrying 12 students to slide off the road and roll in the ditch 5 miles west of Sioux Center, however, no injuries occurred. Accumulations reached 6.5 inches at Orange City and 6.0 inches at Rock Valley. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system moved across the midwest, bringing severe storms to some and wintry weather to others. Precipitation across Iowa began late on the 9th as rain, with much of the area in the warm sector of the cyclone. As the cold front passed across the state temperature dropped in the early morning hours before stalling. Temperatures across northern Iowa hovered around freezing and eventually elevated surfaces cooled enough for a transition to freezing rain. By early afternoon portions of northern Iowa saw a quarter inch or more of ice accumulation, resulting in tree damage and some power outages. Thankfully warm road temperatures mitigated travel impacts and roads were never reported to accumulate any ice at all. After a break in precipitation during the afternoon, light snow fell across northern Iowa later that night in the deformation zone with minor accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a quarter inch of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front across Kansas and Missouri lifted north into Iowa the evening of September 5th, fueled by the increasing low-level jet. Shortly after midnight on the 6th, the front was situated over northern Iowa and convection out of Minnesota moved southeast into Iowa along the front and into a favorable convective environment as MUCAPE values rose between 2000 to 3000 J/kg. Steep mid level lapse rates of 8 C/km and deep layer shear of 50+ knots allowed for convection to quickly reach severe criteria. Golf ball sized hail and significant wind damage occured with these overnight storms. They were also efficient rain producers, with locations reporting between two to over 4 inches of rain in the heaviest cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL relayed a public report of golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system moved across the midwest, bringing severe storms to some and wintry weather to others. Precipitation across Iowa began late on the 9th as rain, with much of the area in the warm sector of the cyclone. As the cold front passed across the state temperature dropped in the early morning hours before stalling. Temperatures across northern Iowa hovered around freezing and eventually elevated surfaces cooled enough for a transition to freezing rain. By early afternoon portions of northern Iowa saw a quarter inch or more of ice accumulation, resulting in tree damage and some power outages. Thankfully warm road temperatures mitigated travel impacts and roads were never reported to accumulate any ice at all. After a break in precipitation during the afternoon, light snow fell across northern Iowa later that night in the deformation zone with minor accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coop observer in Algona reported 6 to 8 inch diameter tree branches down with a few larger 12 to 14 inch diameter branches down with flickering power. Ice accumulation at 7 am was an eighth to a quarter inch and continued to accumulate through the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front across Kansas and Missouri lifted north into Iowa the evening of September 5th, fueled by the increasing low-level jet. Shortly after midnight on the 6th, the front was situated over northern Iowa and convection out of Minnesota moved southeast into Iowa along the front and into a favorable convective environment as MUCAPE values rose between 2000 to 3000 J/kg. Steep mid level lapse rates of 8 C/km and deep layer shear of 50+ knots allowed for convection to quickly reach severe criteria. Golf ball sized hail and significant wind damage occured with these overnight storms. They were also efficient rain producers, with locations reporting between two to over 4 inches of rain in the heaviest cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clarion airport AWOS reported a 51 knot gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of significant precipitation as the agricultural season ended maintained severe to extreme drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained extreme (D3) through November. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains.  An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation.  More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon.  Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain overnight and through the morning resulted in ice accumulation of a quarter to half inch, especially on elevated surfaces, including powerlines.  Brief power outages were reported.  Snowfall developed later morning and through the afternoon, and accumulated to 3.0 inches at Sioux City.  Slick roadways resulted in several accidents. An (indirect) fatality occurred on eastbound U.S. Highway 20 west of Moville shortly after 1200CST, when the driver and sole occupant of the vehicle left the road and rolled several times.  Around the onset time of the freezing rain during the evening of November 9, more than 200 ducks were killed when they confused wet and ice-covered Highway 20 for wetlands and were hit by passing vehicles. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of severe thunderstorms produced a derecho which tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Monday, August 10th, resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. The cost of damage from this storm was extreme, reaching an estimated 7.5 billion dollars along the path of these storms from Iowa through Illinois.  Two brief tornadoes have been confirmed within the widespread swath of wind damage, one just north of the Eastern Iowa Airport in Linn County Iowa, and one just south of Freeport, Illinois. These tornadoes have been designated as EF-U, as there was no observable damage directly attributable to the tornadoes from which an EF-scale rating could be assigned. While an average of two derechos a year occur across the area, a derecho of this intensity is a roughly once-in-a-decade occurrence. Another unusual aspect of this derecho was the duration of the strong winds, with gusts over 60 mph for over an hour at some locations.||The most extreme winds, estimated at 110-140 mph, destroyed or damaged numerous outbuildings, barns, grain bins, homes, mobile homes, apartment buildings, trees, and power poles in parts of Benton, Linn, Jones, Cedar, and Clinton Counties. The Cedar Rapids area was particularly hard hit. One fatality occurred in Linn County, as a tree fell on a cyclist. Several homes, apartment complexes, and businesses sustained damage consistent with 130-140 mph winds. Radio transmission towers in Marion and Clinton, Iowa collapsed due to winds estimated around 130 mph. Wind gusts of 80-100 mph were common as the line of storms moved through the Quad Cities area and then through northwest Illinois. A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread straight-line winds that produced extensive damage were reported throughout Benton County, associated with a derecho. These winds lasted around an hour in total, even though the initial line of convection moved out fast. Damaging straight-line winds continued and were associated with the rear inflow jet. Maximum wind speeds were reported up to 80 mph for much of the county, with areas in central Benton County that had wind speed estimates from 120 mph wind or greater. The maximum measured unofficial wind gust was 126 mph in Atkins, IA. These estimates were determined based off damage surveys and photos submitted through social media. These strong winds extensively damaged most trees, powerlines, crops, and many structures in their path. This resulted in long duration power outages.

There was no damage directly attributable to the tornado from which an EF-scale rating could be assigned. Thus, the tornado is designated EF-U.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains.  An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation.  More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon.  Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet, freezing rain and freezing drizzle overnight and much of the morning accumulated ice to around a tenth of an inch, especially on elevated surfaces. Precipitation changed to snow during the afternoon, briefly heavy between 1300-1600CST. Snowfall totaled 5.6 inches at Sanborn, 4.8 inches at Sheldon, and 3.6 inches at Primghar.

The Cedar Rapids area was particularly hard hit. One fatality occurred in Linn County, as a tree fell on a cyclist. Several homes, apartment complexes, and businesses sustained damage consistent with 130-140 mph winds. Radio transmission towers in Marion and Clinton, Iowa collapsed due to winds estimated around 130 mph. Wind gusts of 80-100 mph were common as the line of storms moved through the Quad Cities area and then through northwest Illinois. A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.

These estimates were determined based off NWS Storm Surveys, damage reports and photos submitted through social media. The duration of strong winds caused extensive damage to most trees, powerlines, crops, and structures in their path. The widespread damage also resulted in long duration power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains.  An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation.  More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon.  Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle overnight and much of the morning accumulated to one to two tenths of an inch, especially on elevated surfaces, including powerlines which resulted in brief power outages.  Precipitation changed to snow during the midday and afternoon, and totaled 2.5 inches 4 miles west northwest of Everly and 1.7 inches at Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developing along a nearly stationary frontal boundary through central South Dakota and Nebraska organized into a line by late evening. The environment with extreme mid-level instability (greater than 9 degrees C/km) and 1200-1500 J/kg DCAPE helped to enhance damaging wind gusts across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped several feet above the ground. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing across central South Dakota and Nebraska ignited severe storms overnight. With elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and an increasing low-level jet, numerous storms formed along the 700 hPa baroclinic zone with a few taking on bowing characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather station KSUX near Sergeant Bluff measured a peak wind of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher flows from rainfall and snow melt were sustained by heavy rainfall again at the end of March and beginning of April. A drier period heading into mid-month allowed the river to fall below flood stage.

Extensive flooding of agricultural land occurred near the river, and the north access road to the Big Sioux Recreation Area was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated thunderstorm developed on the combined boundary of a cold front and outflow from earlier night convection. A strong updraft congealed and resulted in spotty large hail from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa.
