EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through toward northern Iowa by mid-afternoon, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary. With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to around 60 mph accompanied ping-pong ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal across northwest Iowa, varying from 10 to 25 percent of normal in the far northwest corner, to around 50 percent of normal toward Ida Grove and Storm Lake.  Temperatures were mainly near to slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained at severe (D2) levels through the month. Very warm and dry conditions at the start of the month were broken by rainfall of almost one inch at Sanborn and Primghar between September 8-12.  However, very little precipitation and continued warm temperatures later in the month sustained the short-term agricultural drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media shows a tree down and other large branches in Anamosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.

A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.72 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point, 1.58 inches at Rock Valley, 1.51 inches at Canton and 1.37 inches at Akron, the Big Sioux River near Richland (gage site BSRS2) rose above flood stage late month. The river crested at 1.62 feet above flood stage on March 31, resulting in significant flooding of agricultural land. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and quick moving cold front made its way through the Midwest. The environment ahead of the cold front was unseasonably warm, with temperatures in the upper 60s and readings in the 40s not far behind the front. This led to strong baroclinicity along the front, and subsequent convection with just the right amount of instability. This is considered a high shear low CAPE (HSLC) event, where organized severe thunderstorms are favored in this environment. Potential hazards are damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes. Six tornadoes were confirmed and several wind reports were received (ranging between 50-60 mph, with a few gusts over 60 mph). Damage reports ranged from structural damage to tree damage. Many residents were left without power in parts of northwestern IL, leading to some schools canceling classes on the 11th. Besides the strong winds, a few severe hail reports were received.||In addition, heavy rain occurred with some of the stronger storms, with rainfall totals over an inch. For how dry it has been, flash flooding was not much of a concern. While the environment was very moist and favorable for heavy rainfall, the fast storm motions led to little accumulation through much of our forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated a wind gust of around 60 mph with the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area bringing with it light snow fall and winds gusting up to 60 mph. Blizzard conditions were observed much of the area. Many other locations not in the blizzard warning, experience near blizzard conditions and had impacts warranting a Winter Storm warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured peak wind of 60 mph. Light snow combined with strong winds led to a reduction of visibility and winter weather impacts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported.  It was close to the ground, but observers very close to it confirmed it did not touch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail varied in size from quarters to golf balls. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall which averaged 1.00 to 1.50 inches around the Sioux Falls area, the Big Sioux River at Brandon (gage site BBSS2) returned to flood late month. The river crested at 0.66 feet above flood stage on March 29, resulting in extensive flooding of agricultural land near the river and flooding the north access road to the Big Sioux Recreation Area. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought heavy rain and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and Illinois the evening of May 25th, 2020.  Flash flooding, and damaging winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported part of a machine shed wall and roof damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains. A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which briefly produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Orange City and 3 miles north of Sioux Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped off near the corner of Mississippi Blvd and 16th Street in Bettendorf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Toronto, Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, producing damaging wind and spotty large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were taken down by the strong winds. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds flattened crops 3 to 4 miles east of Milford. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough developed across the Northern Plains and locked in the cold air which had pushed southward and produced numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures. As an extended period of warm advection and frontogenesis intensified during the afternoon of October 24, a broad area of snowfall developed, with periods of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon of October 25. Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures, which also propelled the month into the top 3 snowiest Octobers at Brookings, Flandreau, Marshall, Minneota, Tracy and Yankton (1st), Windom and De Smet (2nd), and Sibley, Sanborn and Pipestone (3rd).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.3 inches 2 miles southwest of Spirit Lake and 3.4 inches at Spirit Lake.

Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures, which also propelled the month into the top 3 snowiest Octobers at Brookings, Flandreau, Marshall, Minneota, Tracy and Yankton (1st), Windom and De Smet (2nd), and Sibley, Sanborn and Pipestone (3rd).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches 3 miles west northwest of Everly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 inch tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.51 inches at Canton and averaged amounts of 1.00 to 1.50 inches around Sioux Falls, the Big Sioux River above Canton (gage site CBSS2) rose to minor flood briefly late month. The river crested at 0.23 feet above flood stage on March 30, resulting in minor flooding of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video sent through social media showing a tree falling in a yard and over a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous round of showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa during the day on the 22nd as a storm system interacted with numerous outflow boundaries and a quasi-stationary front across the area. These storms brought heavy rains of 1 to 2 inches in Benton and Buchanan Counties that caused flooding, as well as severe wind gusts in southeast Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An east/west mesoscale snow band developed across central Iowa from the late morning through early afternoon hours.  Snowfall rates became intense - at times 1 to 3��� per hour.  Snowfall amounts were significant - with a narrow axis (5 to 10 miles wide)  from Guthrie Center, to Grimes to Ankeny to Bondurant picking up 5-8���  In addition visibility dropped from 1/2SM to below 1/4SM at times.  With road temperatures above freezing ahead of the front, there was also a rapid snow accumulation that took place.  Thankfully winds were not strong, generally out of the east from 5 to 15 mph. The squall resulted in a number of car accidents across the Des Moines metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. Visibility dropped below one quarter mile from approximately 1035am to 1215pm local time per Ankeny Airport KIKV. Snowfall rates of one to three inches per hour occurred in a narrow band with roads becoming snow and slush covered. Several multi vehicle accidents were reported between 1139am and 117pm local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across northeast Iowa on June 9th. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally higher totals of 4 to 5 inches. This heavy rain created flash flooding across portions of Fayette, Chickasaw and Howard Counties. Numerous roads were closed because of high water across Fayette County including State Highways 3 and 150. Roads were also closed west of Protovin (Howard County) and east of Lawler (Chickasaw County). The highest reported rainfall amounts were 5.25 inches east of Oelwein (Fayette County) and 4.6 inches west of Maynard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just east of the Oelwein area, 5.25 inches of rain was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several more rounds of heavy rain across the Wapsipinicon River basin during the last week of June brought a third round of major river flooding to the Wapsipinicon River near De Witt. This flooding continued into the early morning hours of July 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wapsipinicon River near De Witt rose above its major flood stage of 12.5 feet around 5 PM CST on June 28 and remained above major flood stage through the end of the month. The river crested at 12.83 feet around 730 PM on June 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into northwest Iowa during the very late afternoon and early evening. As larger-scale and deeper lift forcing with the upper-level jet pushed out across the frontal boundary toward early evening, scattered thunderstorms in the environment of 30-35 knots deep-layer shear and around 1000 J/kg of MLCAPE quickly became linear with mainly damaging wind and spotty hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was accompanied by winds estimated at less than 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened early in the month due to very warm temperatures and lack of precipitation. Extremely low-moisture fuels in the form of dormant grasses and unharvested and cured crops were in place as southerly winds reached a gusty 30 to 50 mph during the afternoon, producing a very high fire danger despite somewhat higher humidity levels.  The fire near Anthon was the largest and most destructive fire of the day, which saw numerous smaller fires in northwest Iowa and surrounding areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 1200LST, a spark from a combine in harvest operations ignited a fire around two miles south of the intersection of county highway D38 and Lee Avenue.  Explosive northward spread occurred due to dry crops and southerly winds which gusted to over 40 mph.  The fire crossed several roads, consuming over 3000 acres of crops, numerous power poles, a semi-truck and a few pieces of farm equipment.  At the peak, around 1200 acres were engulfed in flame. Area farmers plowed fire breaks, and fire departments from seven surrounding communities were activated to protect residences. Property damage amounts are rough estimates.  Crop damage estimates computed from crop value and destroyed acreage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side low developed over eastern Colorado with a warm front across Kansas and Missouri. The front lifted north into Iowa through the day, allowing for thunderstorm development south of the warm front in the warm sector and west along the cold front. SBCAPE values were near 2000 J/kg with deep layer shear of 40-50+ knots. There was a narrow window between warm front and cold front passage for a favorable severe weather environment, but a few strong storms were able to develop with a few reports of quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL relayed a public report and picture of roughly quarter sized hail near Voorhies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Cristobal tracked across much of the mid and upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning and afternoon of June 9, 2020. Very high levels of moisture were present, bringing very heavy rainfall to much of the region, especially eastern Iowa. Rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches fell roughly west of a line from Keosauqua IA, to Burlington IA, to Guttenberg IA. Localized amounts of 4-5 inches were reported near Belle Plaine, IA.||This torrential rainfall led to numerous reports of flooded roadways, road closures and creeks out of the their banks. In addition, area rivers also showed response, with some rising near to well above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported flash flooding of the West Branch of Buffalo Creek at 652 PM CDT. Additionally, there are numerous reports of road closures and water over the roadways  in the northern Half of Buchanan County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area river gauges were indicating high water leavels occurring on many creeks and rivers across Johnson County and in particular in North Liberty and Iowa City. While levels have peaked and creeks and rivers are starting to recede, these creeks and rivers remain out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued below normal across northwest Iowa, varying from 50 to 75 percent of normal. Temperatures were well-below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions reached extreme (D3) in early October, and remained at that level through the remainder of the month. A majority of precipitation at Rock Rapids occurred around October 11-12, with the rest of the month quite dry.  Temperatures were over five degrees below normal for the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression Cristobal tracked across much of the mid and upper Mississippi River Valley on the morning and afternoon of June 9, 2020. Very high levels of moisture were present, bringing very heavy rainfall to much of the region, especially eastern Iowa. Rainfall amounts of 2-4 inches fell roughly west of a line from Keosauqua IA, to Burlington IA, to Guttenberg IA. Localized amounts of 4-5 inches were reported near Belle Plaine, IA.||This torrential rainfall led to numerous reports of flooded roadways, road closures and creeks out of the their banks. In addition, area rivers also showed response, with some rising near to well above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a public report that Heney Creek was over the bridge on 140th avenue southwest of Koszta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous round of showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa during the day on the 22nd as a storm system interacted with numerous outflow boundaries and a quasi-stationary front across the area. These storms brought heavy rains of 1 to 2 inches in Benton and Buchanan Counties that caused flooding, as well as severe wind gusts in southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that a nearby creek is out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low to the northwest of Iowa allowed for convection to develop in the warm sector and along the surface boundary as it pushed across northern Iowa through the afternoon and evening of the 21st. These storms caused damaging winds and large hail across northern and central Iowa. Localized areas received heavy rain that resulted in flooding overnight. Another shortwave embedded within the parent longwave trough allowed for additional rain across the state on the 22nd that lasted through the afternoon. This second round of rain exacerbated the ongoing flooded areas, resulting in flooding that lingered into the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported that lightning hit a walnut tree 100 ft. from the house and fried the well pump.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough across the northern plains allowed for a region of broad ascent to support scattered convection across the area on the morning of June 26th. This activity remained sub-severe with the exception of a suspected microburst on the south side of the Des Moines metro which resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. As the trough pushed east a surface boundary moved across the state later in the day. While this also remained sub-severe, localized pockets of heavy rain did develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports of large tree limbs down across the area, some large enough to fold a chain link fence. Thousands were without power, which was restored by 830a. Radar data indicates likely microburst.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report from Albia of 4.37 inches of rain between 745p and 11p with heavy rain still falling at time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed in the vicinity of a surface boundary across central Iowa. Tall and skinny CAPE profiles were not supportive of severe weather but did allow for a couple of funnel cloud reports across central Iowa. Localized pockets of heavy rain resulted in areas receiving nearly 2 inches of rain in an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report via social media of 1.9 inches of rain between 4:45pm and 5:45pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Manager reported multiple large trees down with power outages. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the morning of June 9 remnants of Tropical Storm Cristobal pushed north into Iowa, drenching central and eastern Iowa for most of the day before lifting into the Great Lakes region by evening. The quick exit of Cristobal was due in part to a strong shortwave moving across the northern plains that helped to kick Cristobal from the area. This secondary system moved into Iowa the evening of June 9th, and while for the most part remained sub-severe, damaging winds were reported in west-central Iowa with a stronger cell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 1.5 inches of rain. Beginning time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough with associated surface low pushed across the northern plains, pulling a cool front into Iowa. Scattered convection developed along the boundary, resulting in damaging wind gusts. A brief EFU tornado also developed over open land in northern Iowa. This system grew upscale overnight, bringing heavy rain to north-central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coop observer reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 2.75 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of June 26th. These storms dropped some hail near Lime Springs (Howard County) that ranged in size from a nickel up to a quarter. These storms also produced wind damage just north of Lime Springs where trees and power lines were blown down and the doors to a metal machine shed were blown onto the roof of the shed. A private weather station measured a 67 mph wind gust in Waucoma (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail fell in Lime Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a large tree branch down, reported via social media. Time estimated via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across northeast Iowa on June 9th. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally higher totals of 4 to 5 inches. This heavy rain created flash flooding across portions of Fayette, Chickasaw and Howard Counties. Numerous roads were closed because of high water across Fayette County including State Highways 3 and 150. Roads were also closed west of Protovin (Howard County) and east of Lawler (Chickasaw County). The highest reported rainfall amounts were 5.25 inches east of Oelwein (Fayette County) and 4.6 inches west of Maynard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just west of Ionia, 3.03 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the morning of June 9 remnants of Tropical Storm Cristobal pushed north into Iowa, drenching central and eastern Iowa for most of the day before lifting into the Great Lakes region by evening. The quick exit of Cristobal was due in part to a strong shortwave moving across the northern plains that helped to kick Cristobal from the area. This secondary system moved into Iowa the evening of June 9th, and while for the most part remained sub-severe, damaging winds were reported in west-central Iowa with a stronger cell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer recorded 3.11 inches of rainfall over the last 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains.  An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation.  More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon.  Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow was intermittently mixed with light freezing rain during the morning with up to a tenth of an inch ice accumulation. Snow continued through afternoon, and was briefly heavy between 1300-1600CST. Snowfall totaled 6.5 inches at Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection across the central plains moved into Iowa during the early morning hours of June 28th. While storms had diminished in strength, a damaging wind gust did occur. The precipitation associated with this mesoscale convective vortex continued to weaken through the morning, however, the environment in its wake exhibited high low-level vorticity that supported funnel cloud development later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two funnel clouds spotted with storms northwest of Lake City, Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thermal ridge across much of the midwest brought the first round of summer-like temperatures to the area in early June. By late in the day a shortwave moving across the northern plains flattened the nose of the ridge a pulled a cool front across northern Iowa. Convection developed along this boundary in the evening in the form of gusty winds across northern Iowa. Convection then diminished overnight as the front continued to sink south across the state. By early morning the front had made it to central Iowa where storms restrengthened, dropping large hail and producing gusty winds. By mid-morning the front had pushed south of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of golf ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet end to the 3rd week of June and continued moderate to heavy rainfalls into the middle of the 4th week of June caused rivers across the eastern portions of the Des Moines CWA to swell and reach flood stage from as little as a few hours near Tama to a few days around Cedar Falls, Waterloo, and Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Cedar Falls crested at 0030 UTC on June 25th at 91.78 feet. Flood stage is 89.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station reported a 24-hour rainfall total of 2.10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thermal ridge across much of the midwest brought the first round of summer-like temperatures to the area in early June. By late in the day a shortwave moving across the northern plains flattened the nose of the ridge a pulled a cool front across northern Iowa. Convection developed along this boundary in the evening in the form of gusty winds across northern Iowa. Convection then diminished overnight as the front continued to sink south across the state. By early morning the front had made it to central Iowa where storms restrengthened, dropping large hail and producing gusty winds. By mid-morning the front had pushed south of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusts of 66 mph reported at Estherville Airport ASOS well in advance of approaching thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough with associated surface low pushed across the northern plains, pulling a cool front into Iowa. Scattered convection developed along the boundary, resulting in damaging wind gusts. A brief EFU tornado also developed over open land in northern Iowa. This system grew upscale overnight, bringing heavy rain to north-central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pictures and one video obtained via social media tracked a tornado across rural Kossuth county.  The tornado produced no impacts to damage indicators, hence the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms rolled across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of June 2nd. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts. Trees were blown down in St. Ansgar and Osage (Mitchell County) and power lines were downed in Stacyville (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and branches were blown down in St. Ansgar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across northeast Iowa on June 9th. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally higher totals of 4 to 5 inches. This heavy rain created flash flooding across portions of Fayette, Chickasaw and Howard Counties. Numerous roads were closed because of high water across Fayette County including State Highways 3 and 150. Roads were also closed west of Protovin (Howard County) and east of Lawler (Chickasaw County). The highest reported rainfall amounts were 5.25 inches east of Oelwein (Fayette County) and 4.6 inches west of Maynard (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rains associated with Tropical Depression Cristobal pushed the Turkey River out of its banks twice in Spillville. The first crest was almost two and half feet above the flood stage at 11.4 feet on the 10th. The second crest was around a quarter of a foot above the flood stage at 9.23 feet, also on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough with associated surface low pushed across the northern plains, pulling a cool front into Iowa. Scattered convection developed along the boundary, resulting in damaging wind gusts. A brief EFU tornado also developed over open land in northern Iowa. This system grew upscale overnight, bringing heavy rain to north-central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 2.25 inches of rain. Beginning and end time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low to the northwest of Iowa allowed for convection to develop in the warm sector and along the surface boundary as it pushed across northern Iowa through the afternoon and evening of the 21st. These storms caused damaging winds and large hail across northern and central Iowa. Localized areas received heavy rain that resulted in flooding overnight. Another shortwave embedded within the parent longwave trough allowed for additional rain across the state on the 22nd that lasted through the afternoon. This second round of rain exacerbated the ongoing flooded areas, resulting in flooding that lingered into the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The department of transportation relayed a report from the Tama county sheriff's office that there was flooding on US 63 south from just north of Traer by the golf course near Wolf Creek. Water was over the southbound lane roughly to the centerline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough ejected east out of the base of a longwave western US trough, lifting across the southern plains and into Iowa. Strong frontogenesis led to a band of heavy snow on the leading edge of the system on the morning of the 29th. A secondary surge of moisture lifted into the area later in the day thanks to a strong 70 knot low level jet. Precipitation began initially as sleet and freezing rain across southern Iowa with a deep warm inversion above the surface, but by midday precipitation transitioned over to all snow. Snow rates exceeded 1+ inches per hour at times through the afternoon and early evening before tapering off later that night.

Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Winds in the lower mixed layer reached 40 to 60 knots as Arctic air surged into the area behind the system, producing numerous hours of widespread whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 54 mph at Luverne and 60 mph at Sheldon, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 3.5 inches 3 miles north of Rock Rapids and 3.0 inches at Rock Rapids resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of northwest IA.

An early season winter storm led to widespread snowfall throughout the area, with totals around 4-6 inches northwest of a line from Iowa City to Dubuque. The heaviest snow was reported near Dubuque, where 8 to 10 inches was reported. Precipitation began as rain with warm, moist air in place across the region. The rain changed over to snow during the evening hours along and Highway 20 corridor.  By 6:00am on the 12th, the snow had spread as far south as Interstate 80.The snow was heavy at times, especially in near where rain was transitioning to snow.  Key impacts: transportation delays and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from 2 to 10 inches, with the highest observed snowfall from the official NWS observation at the Dubuque Regional Airport, with 9.4 inches. Heaviest amounts within the county were generally centered near the Dubuque area, in eastern Dubuque County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm produced widespread heavy snow, along with freezing rain and sleet, across the region in the afternoon of the 29th and into the early morning hours of the 30th. 8 to 12 inches of snow fell in the heaviest band between Interstate 80 and U.S. Highway 20. The highest freezing rain accumulations occurred along the U.S. Highway 34 corridor from Fairfield, IA to Oquawka, IL, where 0.2 to 0.3 inches of glaze ice was reported. Thunderstorms also occurred in eastern Iowa Tuesday evening, and resulted in extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour. Key impacts: transportation delays and traffic accidents, along with some isolated power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official observation at the Dubuque Regional Airport was 7.4 inches. A trained spotter reported 5.8 inches 1 miles south of Edmore.

The highest freezing rain accumulations occurred along the U.S. Highway 34 corridor from Fairfield, IA to Oquawka, IL, where 0.2 to 0.3 inches of glaze ice was reported. Thunderstorms also occurred in eastern Iowa Tuesday evening, and resulted in extreme snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour. Key impacts: transportation delays and traffic accidents, along with some isolated power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 6.0 inches from a trained spotter in Durant to 10.5 inches from the COOP observer in Lowden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous winter system impacted the area, resulting in rapidly falling temperatures with the cold front passage, strong winds statewide regularly gusting to or over 50 mph, and blizzard conditions in northwest Iowa where snow accumulation was greatest. Synoptically, two areas of low pressure merged across northern Iowa and lifted northeast, pulling a cold front across the area. Precipitation developed along the initial cold front, though better moisture and additional snow fell within the deformation zone of the system. Winds remained elevated during this time, resulting in widespread diminished visibility across northern Iowa much of the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains. A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which briefly produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.5 inches at Akron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough ejected east out of the base of a longwave western US trough, lifting across the southern plains and into Iowa. Strong frontogenesis led to a band of heavy snow on the leading edge of the system on the morning of the 29th. A secondary surge of moisture lifted into the area later in the day thanks to a strong 70 knot low level jet. Precipitation began initially as sleet and freezing rain across southern Iowa with a deep warm inversion above the surface, but by midday precipitation transitioned over to all snow. Snow rates exceeded 1+ inches per hour at times through the afternoon and early evening before tapering off later that night.

Severe thunderstorms tracked across much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois in the early afternoon hours of May 23rd, 2020. Storms quickly intensified in an extremely favorable environment, which was the result of a warm front lifting north across the Midwest. Seven tornadoes were confirmed from this event across Iowa and Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A  tornado developed southwest of Mechanicsville shortly before 1 pm, and tracked northeast through northern Cedar County. The tornado crossed into Jones County about 3 miles S of Morley before dissipating. The tornado was rated EF1 in Cedar County with maximum winds estimated around 95 mph. The tornado is designated as EF-U in Jones County as there was no observable damage. The tornado was on the ground for about 16 minutes along an intermittent 7 mile path. Assistance with the survey was provided by Cedar County Emergency Management and the Mechanicsville Fire Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across central Iowa into eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and northwest Illinois late Friday afternoon and into the overnight hours. These storms brought heavy rain, frequent lightning, damaging winds, and large hail to the region.  The hardest hit areas were Keokuk county in Iowa, towards Henry county in Iowa, where 50-70 mph winds occurred.  A weak EF-1 tornado occurred near Atkinson, IL in Henry county in Illinois after 1015 pm and caused damage to trees and outbuildings at two farmsteads. ||Widespread rainfall amounts over 1 inch generally fell south of a line from Sigourney and Maquoketa, IA to Freeport, IL.  This rainfall combined with the previous days rain brought rain totals of 2-4 inches to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported blown down. A machine shed was damaged and blown onto a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois in the early afternoon hours of May 23rd, 2020. Storms quickly intensified in an extremely favorable environment, which was the result of a warm front lifting north across the Midwest. Seven tornadoes were confirmed from this event across Iowa and Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado caused damage at a farmstead in Johnson county, and crossed into Cedar County just south of the Cedar River, around 3.5 miles NW of Cedar Bluff. The tornado downed power poles east of Sutliff. The tornado was rated as EF-0 in Johnson County, with maximum winds of 85 mph. The rating in Cedar County was EF-1, with maximum winds around 110 mph. Johnson and Cedar County Emergency Management provided assistance in completing the survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across much of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois in the early afternoon hours of May 23rd, 2020. Storms quickly intensified in an extremely favorable environment, which was the result of a warm front lifting north across the Midwest. Seven tornadoes were confirmed from this event across Iowa and Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rotating wall cloud was reported, with a funnel that did not touch the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front stalled across the Minnesota and Iowa border for a few days finally made a push across the state, providing a focus for convective development. Deep layer shear of 30 to 40 knots and CAPE over 2000 J/kg were sufficient for storm development. As the line of storms moved across the state with the front, several hail cores developed resulting in quarter sized hail in east central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported mostly dime sized hail with a few stones up to quarter sized, accompanied by estimated 40 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave moved across the state, and while CAPE values were bewteen 2000-3000 J/kg limited deep layer shear made it difficult for any significant convection to develop. Isolated storms developed across northern Iowa in the early evening, however they were unable to strengthen to severe limits. With steep low level lapse rates and sufficient low level shear, storms did manage to develop two funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a funnel cloud west of Pocahontas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moved across the Dakotas and southeast towards Iowa, increasing warm air advection and moisture return into Iowa ahead of the low. Scattered convection developed by mid morning across western Iowa with a few cells strengthening enough to drop hail in Carrol and Guthrie counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of quarter to ping pong ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minimal rainfall during the summer led to the continued deterioration of conditions across portions of west central into central Iowa.  Extreme drought conditions developed in early August and remained for the rest of the month as little rainfall occurred.  August ended up being the 3rd driest on record across Iowa with slightly above normal temperatures.  Along with maturation of field crops, soil moisture became very low with small streams drying up by the end of the month in many locations in the extreme area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions since late spring and the continuation of these conditions in August led to the expansion of severe to extreme drought conditions in Greene county during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Basic Summary: A cluster of storms in northeast Nebraska and southeast South Dakota developed into the destructive derecho that raced eastward across large portions of Iowa, northern Illinois, southern Wisconsin, northern Indiana, and southern Michigan during the morning and afternoon of August 10th. The strongest winds were experienced in central and east-central Iowa where 100+ mph winds were observed. Some of the strongest winds were in an area from roughly Marshalltown, IA eastward along the Highway 30 corridor to the Mississippi River, where estimated winds exceeded 120+ mph and approached 140 mph. A number of QLCS tornadoes were also experienced within the Highway 30 corridor. One of the most remarkable aspects of the derecho was the duration of severe winds, with many areas experiencing at least periodic, if not persistent, severe level (58+ mph) winds over a roughly 30 minute time frame. As the derecho moved eastward out of Iowa, it continued to expand, but weakened from its peak strength. Winds continued to exceed severe criteria (58+ mph) in Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, and Michigan and began to produce additional QLCS tornadoes across northern Illinois before fading out across northern Indiana and southern Michigan.||In the wake of the derecho, damage was substantial in many areas, rural and urban. Many agriculture fields, mainly corn, were flattened within the swaths of strongest winds. Initial estimates suggest at least 850,000 acres of partial or complete crop loss occurred from this event. Within urban and wooded areas extensive tree and structure damage was seen, including complete roof failure in a few instances. At its peak, total estimated customers without power exceeded 1.4 million across the affected states. In total, early estimates on losses (agriculture, infrastructure, private property) have exceeded $7.5 billion across the states affected. ||Environment: Broadly speaking, it was a ripe day for thunderstorms across the affected area with MUCAPE values often exceeding 3500-4000 J/kg by the mid morning hours concurrent with mid-upper level short wave support, and indications that any cold pool development would be strong (difference between cold pool and ambient air) and potentially long lived. For detailed environmental analysis, including soundings, please view the various local NWS office event review pages: NWS Des Moines, NWS Quad Cities, NWS Chicago, NWS Milwaukee, NWS Central Illinois, NWS Northern Indiana, or NWS Indianapolis.||Additional Details: Numerous injuries occurred during the derecho and in the aftermath during cleanup, including 4 direct and indirect deaths of which 3 occurred in Iowa (two in Poweshiek County and one in Linn County) and 1 in Indiana (Fort Wayne).||Disclaimer: The August 10, 2020 derecho resulted in widespread high-end damage across large portions of central and southern Iowa. Given the size and scope of the event and understanding that final damage and loss estimates (agriculture, infrastructure, and private property) will take months to complete, the NWS is not able to estimate the monetary impact at this time. Values may be added as more data becomes from various sources in future months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large tree limb 6 inches in diameter and 20 feet long was downed. Several smaller limbs were downed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minimal rainfall during the summer led to the continued deterioration of conditions across portions of west central into central Iowa.  Extreme drought conditions developed in early August and remained for the rest of the month as little rainfall occurred.  August ended up being the 3rd driest on record across Iowa with slightly above normal temperatures.  Along with maturation of field crops, soil moisture became very low with small streams drying up by the end of the month in many locations in the extreme area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions since late spring and the continuation of these conditions in August led to the expansion of severe to extreme drought conditions in Calhoun county during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Basic Summary: A cluster of storms in northeast Nebraska and southeast South Dakota developed into the destructive derecho that raced eastward across large portions of Iowa, northern Illinois, southern Wisconsin, northern Indiana, and southern Michigan during the morning and afternoon of August 10th. The strongest winds were experienced in central and east-central Iowa where 100+ mph winds were observed. Some of the strongest winds were in an area from roughly Marshalltown, IA eastward along the Highway 30 corridor to the Mississippi River, where estimated winds exceeded 120+ mph and approached 140 mph. A number of QLCS tornadoes were also experienced within the Highway 30 corridor. One of the most remarkable aspects of the derecho was the duration of severe winds, with many areas experiencing at least periodic, if not persistent, severe level (58+ mph) winds over a roughly 30 minute time frame. As the derecho moved eastward out of Iowa, it continued to expand, but weakened from its peak strength. Winds continued to exceed severe criteria (58+ mph) in Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, and Michigan and began to produce additional QLCS tornadoes across northern Illinois before fading out across northern Indiana and southern Michigan.||In the wake of the derecho, damage was substantial in many areas, rural and urban. Many agriculture fields, mainly corn, were flattened within the swaths of strongest winds. Initial estimates suggest at least 850,000 acres of partial or complete crop loss occurred from this event. Within urban and wooded areas extensive tree and structure damage was seen, including complete roof failure in a few instances. At its peak, total estimated customers without power exceeded 1.4 million across the affected states. In total, early estimates on losses (agriculture, infrastructure, private property) have exceeded $7.5 billion across the states affected. ||Environment: Broadly speaking, it was a ripe day for thunderstorms across the affected area with MUCAPE values often exceeding 3500-4000 J/kg by the mid morning hours concurrent with mid-upper level short wave support, and indications that any cold pool development would be strong (difference between cold pool and ambient air) and potentially long lived. For detailed environmental analysis, including soundings, please view the various local NWS office event review pages: NWS Des Moines, NWS Quad Cities, NWS Chicago, NWS Milwaukee, NWS Central Illinois, NWS Northern Indiana, or NWS Indianapolis.||Additional Details: Numerous injuries occurred during the derecho and in the aftermath during cleanup, including 4 direct and indirect deaths of which 3 occurred in Iowa (two in Poweshiek County and one in Linn County) and 1 in Indiana (Fort Wayne).||Disclaimer: The August 10, 2020 derecho resulted in widespread high-end damage across large portions of central and southern Iowa. Given the size and scope of the event and understanding that final damage and loss estimates (agriculture, infrastructure, and private property) will take months to complete, the NWS is not able to estimate the monetary impact at this time. Values may be added as more data becomes from various sources in future months.
